nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated, found over coral rubble and reef slopes at depths of about 6–40 m. Often observed in small loose groups of up to roughly 10 individuals.

diet.notes: Has been documented in the wild feeding on small invertebrates and benthic algae. In captivity generally accepts spirulina, marine algae, nori, high-quality angelfish preparations, mysis shrimp, and other small meaty frozen foods. Feeding 2–3 times daily in a tank with established live rock for supplemental grazing tends to give the best results.

avoidWith: large aggressive tankmates that may harass it

imageCandidates.notes: Philippe Bourjon, 1200x939. Tack-sharp in-situ shot showing the whole fish with the diagnostic bright orange dorsal saddle over a deep blue-purple body and blue-ringed eye; best diagnostic clarity.
Brian Gratwicke, 1024x683. Sharp aquarium shot, vivid orange back over deep blue body, full fish in frame; good alternate angle.
Ewout Knoester, 952x714. Whole fish, strong coloration and visible blue eye-ring; slightly softer than the pick, kept for variety.

summary: A small, vivid dwarf angelfish from the Western Indian Ocean, recognizable by a bright orange-yellow dorsal saddle over a deep blue body. Generally considered reef-safe with caution and one of the more manageable Centropyge species.
careNotes: The Flameback Angelfish (often sold as African Pygmy or Orangeback Angelfish) is among the smaller and more manageable Centropyge species, with adults typically reaching only 6–8 cm. A mature aquarium with plentiful live rock for grazing and several caves or overhangs for refuge is generally advised, along with open swimming space — these dwarf angels are active swimmers that tend to dart between rockwork (hence the trade nickname 'Jumping Bean'). It is best introduced last in a peaceful community to limit territorial behaviour, and is typically kept singly unless a confirmed mated pair is available. Reef compatibility is generally good but cannot be guaranteed; occasional nipping at SPS polyps (small-polyped stony corals), large-polyped stony corals, or clam mantles has been reported, so monitoring is worthwhile when housed in a reef.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding is rarely reported and considered difficult; sequential hermaphroditism (with documented bi-directional sex change in this species) means dominant individuals can shift between sexes depending on social structure. In the wild, spawning has been observed at sunset, with pelagic eggs released into the water column and no parental care of larvae. Hobbyist-level captive rearing is not commercially established.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary source for husbandry parameters (tank size, water parameters, temperament, diet, reef-safety, category).

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific name (Centropyge acanthops Norman, 1922), family (Pomacanthidae), order (Acanthuriformes), max size (8.0 cm TL vs LiveAquaria 7.6 cm — close agreement), temperature (24.6–27.8°C wild vs LiveAquaria 22–26°C captive — used 23–27°C average), native range (Western Indian Ocean: Somalia to South Africa), depth (6–40 m), and reproductive biology (bi-directional sex change confirmed).

fish.breedingNotes: Sequential hermaphrodite with documented bi-directional sex change, meaning dominant individuals can shift between sexes depending on social structure. Spawning is pelagic, with eggs released into the water column; captive rearing has not been commercially achieved.
